highlights:
Bayon stands out with its circular layout and face towers, breaking away from the traditional square symmetry of  ...    ### Key features:

Three levels:  ...    ## The Faces of the Bayon These smiling faces, with half-closed eyes, embody transcendent serenity. Their meaning remains debated: - Expression of the king’s benevolent watchfulness over the cardinal directions. - Idealized portrait of Jayavarman VII, deified through the bodhisattva. - Representations of Lokeshvara, embodiment of universal compassion. This sculptural style has become iconic of late Khmer art.

## The Bas-Reliefs: History and Myth  ...    ## Religious Symbolism

The Bayon expresses a Buddhist cosmology centered on the king: - The faces’ orientation implies a divine presence in all directions, an omniscient and benevolent power. - The temple embodies a mandala of a balanced universe, with the king at its center. - The towers represent the thousand faces of compassion watching over the kingdom.  ... stuck. The temple sits at the center of Angkor Thom, with roads going from it in cardinal directions. The temple aligns to the east and sits back inside three enclosures. The temple has no mote or protective walls, as the city's outer mote and fortified wall serve as protection. The third and outermost enclosure contains extensive galleries of**bas-reliefs**(flat sculptures carved into a wall). The second enclosure contains more galleries along the corners  ...    The faces on the towers look out in all directions.

title: Bayon
highlights: many towers adorned with serene, smiling stone faces carved on all sides, which are  ...    The temple is oriented towards the east, and so its buildings are set back to the west inside enclosures elongated along the east-west axis. Because the temple sits at the exact centre of Angkor Thom, roads lead to it directly from the gates at each of the city's cardinal points. The temple itself has no wall or  ...    ### The upper terrace: 200 faces of Lokesvara  ...    The upper terrace is home to the famous "face towers" of the Bayon, each of which supports two, three or (most commonly) four gigantic smiling faces.
